summary: Joel Spolsky breaks down the energy expenditure of shipping commercial software, arguing that writing code is only about 2% of the total effort. The bulk goes to customer service, debugging, marketing, and hiring. He details the non-code work required to launch FogBugz 4.0, including professional graphic design, an online demo, a product movie, a marketing website, a published book, and physical CD-ROMs. The post illustrates how consumers evaluate products based on superficial signals like aesthetics, reputation, and tangible artifacts rather than the software itself. Spolsky argues this explains both why programmer-founded companies often fail and why expensive shelfware succeeds.
key_points: Only about 2% of the energy in shipping commercial software goes to writing new code; the rest is customer service, debugging, marketing, hiring, and non-shipping code.
Consumers evaluate software products through proxy signals like website quality, reputation, community discussion, and aesthetics rather than the product's actual functionality.
People ridiculously overvalue aesthetics when evaluating products, creating an enormous handicap for functional but ugly software.
Making a product 'feel real' requires tangible artifacts like published books, physical CD-ROMs, and professional design, even if few customers use them.
Programmer-founded software companies often fail because programmers only know how to do the 2% (writing code) and lack skills in marketing, customer service, and design.
money_quotes: A ridiculously small portion of the energy it takes to make a commercial software product actually goes into the writing of lines of code.

This, I think, helps explain why so many software companies started by programmers fail: programmers are really good at writing news line of code, they might be good at debugging, but nobody ever taught them how to do marketing or customer service, and they are probably horrible at graphic design.
Oh, and if they have a few minutes left over, they might actually look at your product itself to see if it works.
Human emotions can be really, really superficial. In particular people ridiculously overvalue aesthetics and beauty when evaluating products.

If you have a product that is functional but not beautiful you have a really, really, really, really, really, really, really, really, really, really big handicap to overcome against the product that is beautiful but maybe not so functional.
A real book from a real publishing house is one of those things that makes a product 'feel' real.
I can't tell why but somehow having the option of a physical product makes the software feel more tangible and therefore worth more money.
key_insight: Shipping successful commercial software is overwhelmingly about non-code work — design, marketing, customer service, and creating tangible artifacts that make the product 'feel real' — which is why programmer-founded companies that focus only on code often fail.
